Name: compression_distortion_tradeoff
Title: Quantifying the Trade-off between Model Compression and Task-Specific Distortion
Short Hypothesis: By treating model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we can derive operational bounds on the bits-per-weight necessary to minimize task-relevant distortion, thereby establishing a systematic approach to optimizing model performance through compression.
Related Work: Previous studies, such as 'Population Risk Improvement with Model Compression' and 'Rate Distortion via Constrained Estimated Mutual Information Minimization', have explored the relationship between compression and generalization but lack a unified quantification of how task-specific distortions impact overall model effectiveness. This proposal distinguishes itself by focusing explicitly on deriving information-theoretic limits related to predictive performance under various compression schemes, which has yet to be comprehensively addressed in existing literature.
Abstract: Model compression is a critical aspect of modern machine learning, enabling the deployment of efficient models in resource-constrained environments. However, the relationship between compression and task performance remains poorly understood. This proposal aims to investigate the trade-off between model compression and task-specific distortion through the lens of rate-distortion theory. We will derive operational bounds on the bits-per-weight necessary to achieve a target level of predictive sufficiency while minimizing task-relevant distortion. By framing model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we will establish a systematic methodology for quantifying the information-theoretic limits of various compression strategies. The results will be validated through experiments on benchmark datasets, and we will explore the implications for model selection, architecture design, and compression techniques. This research is expected to provide new insights into the optimal allocation of bits in machine learning models, guiding future efforts in model efficiency and performance optimization.
Experiments: Conduct experiments on standard datasets (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR-10) using various model architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers) and apply different compression techniques (e.g., pruning, quantization).
Quantify the task-specific distortion for each compressed model by evaluating the predictive performance on a held-out test set.
Use information-theoretic metrics (e.g., mutual information between input and output) to assess the trade-off between model size (bits-per-weight) and predictive accuracy.
Establish baseline comparisons with existing compression methods to validate the proposed bounds and trade-offs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of accurately estimating the task-specific distortion and mutual information, which may require careful design of experiments and assumptions. Additionally, the findings may vary significantly across different types of models and tasks, necessitating further investigation into the generalizability of the results.
